data.title: ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding reports Q1 results
data.description:
• ZEEKR Intelligent Technology Holding press release (NYSE:ZK): Q1  Excluding share-based compensation expenses, adjusted net loss was RMB640 million (US$88 million)
  • Total revenues were RMB22,019 million (US$3,034 million) for the first quarter of 2025, representing an increase of 1.1% from the first quarter of 2024 and a decrease of 37.8% from the fourth quarter of 2024.
  • Shares -0.63% PM.

data.title: Rubicon Organics appoints Glen Ibbott as its interim CFO
data.description:
• Rubicon Organics (OTCQX:ROMJF) said it has appointed Glen Ibbott as its interim chief financial officer.
  • The firm said its current CFO, Janis Risbin, will be stepping away from her full-time role effective on Thursday.
  • Ibbott is an industry veteran with over 25 years of leadership experience in publicly traded life sciences and cannabis companies. 
  • The company said it will be initiating a comprehensive search process to identify a permanent CFO.

data.title: Capital One credit card delinquencies, net charge-offs dip in April
data.description:
Capital One Financial's (NYSE:COF) credit card delinquency and net charge-off rates both declined markedly in April even as the company's lending increased.

Its delinquency rate dropped to 3.95% from 4.25% in March 2025 and 4.23% in last year's April. The rate, though, still remains above the 3.42% level from April 2019, before the pandemic disrupted the economy.

The net charge-off rate dipped to 5.66% from 6.09% in March and 6.07% in April 2024. That metric, too, stayed above the prepandemic level of 5.15%.

Lending activity edged up 0.2% M/M and 4.4% Y/Y to $150.6B at April 30, 2025.

data.title: Gambling.com Non-GAAP EPS of $0.46 beats by $0.23, revenue of $40.64M beats by $0.57M
data.description:
• Gambling.com press release (NASDAQ:GAMB): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.46 beats by $0.23.
  • Revenue of $40.64M (+39.1% Y/Y) beats by $0.57M.
  • Gambling.com Group today reiterated the 2025 full-year revenue and Adjusted EBITDA guidance originally provided on February 19, 2025. The Company expects full year revenue of $170 million to $174 million and Adjusted EBITDA of $67 million to $69 million. The midpoints of the new full year revenue and Adjusted EBITDA guidance ranges represent year-over-year growth of 35% and 40%, respectively, and an Adjusted EBITDA margin of 39.5%.

data.title: Synchrony credit card delinquency rate ticks down, net charge-offs tick up
data.description:
Synchrony Financial's (NYSE:SYF) credit card delinquency and net charge-off rates moved in opposite directions in April, albeit slowly. The company also lent less money than it did in the prior month and a year ago.

The delinquency rate of 4.3% moved down from 4.5% in March and 4.6% in April 2024. The credit measure is modestly above its April 2019 rate of 4.1%.

Its net charge-off rate, meanwhile, crept up to 6.3% from 6.2% in the prior month and still remains below 6.7% in the year-ago period. It's also below its prepandemic level (April 2019) of 6.5%.

Lending activity slowed, falling to $99.5B of loan receivables at April 30, 2025, down 0.1% M/M and 2.1% Y/Y.

data.title: Walmart beats top-line and bottom-line estimates; initiates Q2 and reaffirms FY outlook
data.description:
• Walmart press release (NYSE:WMT): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.61 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $165.6B (+2.5% Y/Y) beats by $1.17B.
  • Global eCommerce sales grew 22%, led by storefulfilled pickup & delivery and marketplace.
  • Global advertising business3 grew 50%, including VIZIO; Walmart Connect in the U.S. up 31%.
  • Membership and other income up 3.7%, including 14.8% growth in membership income.
  • Operating cash flow of $5.4 billion, an increase of $1.2 billion.
  • Free cash flow1 of $0.4 billion, an increase of $0.9 billion.
  • Q226: Revenue: $173.47B consensus vs. estimated growth of 3.86% Y/Y. Non-GAAP EPS: $0.70 consensus.
  • FY26: Revenue: $701.89B consensus vs. estimated growth of 4.05% Y/Y (prior increase 3% to 4%). Non-GAAP EPS: $2.61 consensus (prior $2.50 to $2.60).
  • Outlook for Q2 and full-year 
  • Shares +0.56% PM.

data.title: Steve Cohen warns of market complacency on tariffs, says stocks look pricey
data.description:
NEW YORK - Billionaire Steve Cohen, founder of Point72 Asset Management and one of Wall Street’s most successful traders, said markets haven’t priced in the possibility of an economic slowdown in the aftermath of the Trump administration's shifting tariff policies.

Speaking at the Sohn Investment Conference in New York on Wednesday, he said U.S. stocks broadly are expensive compared with earnings power, though he acknowledged that there are differences among industry groups. 

He also estimated the likelihood of a U.S. recession at about 45%.

“We aren’t in a recession yet, but we have significant slowing growth,” he said.

He doesn’t foresee the Federal Reserve cutting interest rates in the near term, citing inflation risks tied to tariffs, he said. U.S. economic growth could decelerate to 1.5% or lower in 2026, “which is OK but not phenomenal," he said.

Reflecting on recent market movements, Cohen described the sharp drop and rebound in April as unusually swift, and said he’s watching closely for how markets respond to the next round of economic data.

“I want to see how the market is going to react and that will tell me a lot about whether we are priced correctly,” he said.

While acknowledging the possibility of a market downturn, he believes any pullback would likely be modest. “Which isn’t a calamity,” he said of a potential 10% to 15% decline, suggesting markets may simply tread water for a period.

Describing the market as "toppy," he said stock indexes are likely to trade sideways amid economic uncertainties.

“Markets don’t have to go up every year,” he said.

Point72 posted a 2.3% gain in April, bringing its return for the year so far to 3% amid turbulent conditions, Bloomberg News reported.

Cohen, who also owns the New York Mets, last year stepped back from running his investment portfolio though he has continued to serve as chairman, chief executive and co-chief investment officer of Point72.

Cohen is one of the most prominent—and polarizing—figures in the investment world. His former firm, SAC Capital Advisors, posted stellar returns from 1993 to 2011, outperforming the market in nearly every year and achieving gains exceeding 60% in some. However, in 2013, SAC pled guilty to insider trading and paid a record $1.8 billion penalty. (Cohen was said to have inspired a key character in the hedge fund drama Billions.)

Following the legal fallout, Cohen rebranded the firm as Point72 and stepped back from managing external capital until 2018, when it reopened to outside investors and secured $5 billion in new funds.

The Sohn Investment Conference for the past 30 years has been newsworthy because it features high-profile hedge fund managers and investors who reveal market views, economic forecasts and sometimes specific stock picks that can influence market sentiment.

Greenlight Capital founder David Einhorn and famed short seller Jim Chanos also appeared on stage at this year's conference. 

The event attracts global financial media attention not just for its investment insights, but also because it raises millions for pediatric cancer research -- lending Wall Street clout with a philanthropic mission. The combination of market-moving commentary and star power makes it a key date on the financial calendar.
